heading: COURT OF INTERNATIONAL TRADE
label: 11 COURT OF INTERNATIONAL TRADE
notes: Historical and Revision NotesThe “Board of General Appraisers” was designated “United States Customs Court” by , . General provisions concerning such court were incorporated in , U.S.C., 1940 ed., Customs Duties, until amended by , , adding a new section to the Judicial Code of 1911, when they were transferred to , U.S.C., 1940 ed. They are retained in title 28 by this revision.act May 28, 1926, ch. 411, § 144 Stat. 669section 1518 of title 19act October 10, 1940, ch. 843, § 154 Stat. 1101section 296 of title 28In this connection former Congressman Walter Chandler said, “Among the major subjects needing study and revision are special courts, such as the Customs Court, which should be fitted into the judicial system.” (See U.S. Law Weekly, .)Nov. 7, 1939

History of CourtThe United States Customs Court [now Court of International Trade] as “constituted on ”, consisted of nine members as provided by , , which established the Board of General Appraisers, designated the “United States Customs Court” by , .June 17, 1930act Sept. 21, 1922, ch. 356, title IV, § 51842 Stat. 972act May 28, 1926, ch. 411, § 144 Stat. 669Provisions similar to these were contained in , . That section was superseded by section 518 of the Tariff Act of 1930, and was repealed by section 651 (a)(1) of said 1930 act.act Sept. 21, 1922, ch. 356, title IV, § 51842 Stat. 972The sentence in the former first paragraph as to sitting in a case previously participated in, is from , , which combined and amended Customs Administrative , , and section 31, as added by , . Section 12 of the act of 1890 was expressly saved from repeal by , , and prior acts, but its provisions, other than the sentence above mentioned, were omitted from the Code.act Aug. 5, 1909, ch. 6, § 2836 Stat. 98Act June 10, 1890, ch. 407, § 1226 Stat. 136act May 27, 1908, ch. 20535 Stat. 406act Sept. 21, 1922, ch. 356, title IV, § 64342 Stat. 989Provisions for the review of decisions of Boards of General Appraisers by the Circuit Courts, made by section 15 of the Customs Administrative , were superseded by provisions for such review by the Court of Customs Appeals created by section 29 added to that act by the Payne-Aldrich Tariff . The provisions of said new section 29 were incorporated in and superseded by chapter 8 of the Judicial Code of , incorporated into the Code as former chapter 8 of Title 28, Judicial Code and Judiciary.Act of June 10, 1890, ch. 407Act of Aug. 5, 1909, ch. 6March 3, 1911R.S. § 2608 provided for the appointment of four appraisers of merchandise, to be employed in visiting ports of entry under the direction of the Secretary of the Treasury, and to assist in the appraisement of merchandise as might be deemed necessary by the Secretary to protect and insure uniformity in the collection of the revenue from customs. It was repealed by , .act June 10, 1890, ch. 407, § 2926 Stat. 141R.S. § 2609 provided for the appointment of merchant appraisers. R.S. § 2610 made every merchant refusing to serve as such appraiser liable to a penalty. Both sections were superseded by the provisions relating to appraisers and appraisements of the Customs Administrative , , and subsequent acts, and were repealed by , .Act of June 10, 1890, ch. 40726 Stat. 131act Sept. 21, 1922, ch. 356, title IV, § 64242 Stat. 989R.S. § 2945, which contained a provision similar to that of R.S. § 2610, was repealed, without mention of section 2610, by said Customs Administrative , , and was again repealed by , .Act of June 10, 1890, ch. 407, § 2926 Stat. 141act Sept. 21, 1922, ch. 356, § 64242 Stat. 989R.S. § 2725, which prescribed the compensation of merchant appraisers, and section 2726, which prescribed the salary of the general appraiser at New York, were superseded by the provisions relating to general appraisers and appraisers made by the Customs Administrative , §§ 12, 13, , as amended by the Payne-Aldrich .Act of June 10, 1890, ch. 40726 Stat. 136Act of Aug. 5, 1909, ch. 6, § 28R.S. § 2727 fixed the salary of the four general appraisers at the sum of $2,500 a year each, and their actual traveling expenses. It was repealed by , .act Feb. 27, 1877, ch. 6919 Stat. 246
